coredata.dc:title: Intensive oxidase-like activity of carbon dots nanozymes modulated with phosphoric acid for sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Guoyong
Wan, Yuqi
Li, Jingkun
Qi, Junjie
Ozaki, Yukihiro
Pi, Fuwei
coredata.dc:description: Development and modulation of carbon dots based nanozymes (CDNs) with pleasing oxidase-like activities remains a challenging and meaningful endeavor. Herein, aiming at enhanced photocatalytic capabilities of CDNs, a phosphoric acid (H3PO4) synthesis strategy was proposed through series of remarkable CDNs development and elaboration, named as P-CDNs. In this modulated strategy, reagent of H3PO4 not only provides P atoms into the carbon dots’ backbone, generating defects for π-electron; but also functionalizes as interbedded phosphate, providing active electron sites to further promote electron efficacy and oxidase-like activities. The capacity of catalytic TMB was used to exemplify the oxidase-like activities of CDNs, and the results confirmed that the catalytic performance of P-CDNs was 3.25 times than that of the reference CDs. Based on the crackajack oxidase-like activity of P-CDNs, a smartphone remote analysis system was readily constructed to sensitive colorimetric/fluorescence dual mode response to diazotization reaction represented by nitrite. Our synthesis strategy not only provides guidance for the design of highly active CDNs but also offers directions for synthetic chemistry and materials science.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Phosphoric acid
Nanozymes
Oxidase-like activity
Nitrite

Characterizations of as-prepared CDs and P-CDNs
Photocatalytic activity of H3PO4 modulated P-CDNs
Enhanced mechanism and effects of P-CDNs100 nanozymes from H3PO4 modulation
Colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode and hydrogel sensor for diazotization reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of as-prepared reference CDs and P-CDNs100. TEM images and size distribution of reference CDs and P-CDNs100 (a–d). Insert: HRTEM images. (e) XRD patterns of the reference CDs and different H3PO4 modulated P-CDNs, (f) Raman spectra (λexc = 532 nm) of reference CDs and different H3PO4 modulated P-CDNs.

Oxidase-like activity characterization of as-prepared the reference CDs and P-CDNs. (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of different reaction systems, (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of P-CDNs100 catalyzed oxidation of TMB, ABTS and OPD. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB catalyzed by reference CDs and P-CDNs. (d) Steady-state kinetic curves of reference CDs and P-CDNs when taking TMB as the substrate. (e) Bursting effect of different free radical trapping agents on the oxidase-like of P-CDNs100. (f) Comparison of UV–vis absorption spectra of P-CDNs100 catalyzing TMB oxidation in different gas environments. (g) The determination of
O 2
· ‐
by EPR based on the catalytic mechanism of PCDNs, with DMPO as the capture agent (h) UV–vis absorption spectra of the reference CDs and P-CDNs, (i) Fluorescence spectra of reference CDs and P-CDNs, emission (λex = 365 nm).

XPS analysis of reference CDs and P-CDNs. N 1s XPS spectra for (a) CDs, (b) P-CDNs20, (c) P-CDNs50, (d) P-CDNs100, (e) Relative ratios of graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N within the structure of the unit material. (f) FT-IR spectra of reference CDs and P-CDNs.

The possible mechanism and active site of P-CDNs oxidase in the TMB system. The modulation of H3PO4 induces more defective sites while exposing more pyrrolic N, in addition, there may be a simultaneous co-promotion of phosphate ions as active sites for the conversion of O2 to
O 2
· ‐
.

Variation of UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the reaction system of TMB + P-CDNs100 with
NO 2 ‐
. (a) UV–vis absorption spectra and color images of TMB + P-CDNs100 reacting with different
NO 2 ‐
concentrations. (b) Schematic illustration of the diazotization reaction of TMB+ with
NO 2 ‐
. (c) Fluorescence spectra and color images of TMB + P-CDNs100 reacting with different
NO 2 ‐
concentrations. (d) The fluorescence lifetime of PCDNs100 +oxTMB system in the presence and absence of
NO 2 ‐
. (e) The UV absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of PCDNs100 +oxTMB+
NO 2 ‐
system. (f) The Stern-Volmer curve of F0/F varying with different NO concentrations. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and color images of TMB + P-CDNs100 reacting with different
NO 2 ‐
. (b) Linear relationship between the A652/A445 value and the logarithm of the different
NO 2 ‐
concentrations. (c) Specificity and anti-interference experiments in colorimetric detection mode, the concentration of
NO 2 ‐
is 100 μM and other interfering substances are 10 times higher than
NO 2 ‐
. (d) Fluorescence spectra of TMB + P-CDNs100 reacting with different
NO 2 ‐
concentrations. (e) Relationship between the fluorescence intensity (F450) and the concentration of
NO 2 ‐
. (f) Specificity and anti-interference experiments in fluorescence detection mode, the concentration of
NO 2 ‐
is 100 μM and other interfering substances are 10 times higher than
NO 2 ‐
. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) The process of manufacturing agarose hydrogel kits. (b) The response mechanism of hydrogel sensors. (c) Process of smartphone on site analysis based on RGB for detection of
NO 2 ‐
. (d) Correlation between RGB color and
NO 2 ‐
concentration. (e) Linear relationship between B/G and
NO 2 ‐
concentration. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Scheme illustration of the synthetic procedure for the H3PO4 P-CDNs and its sensing mechanism toward analytes of nitrite. (a) The synthesis process of P-CDNs and TMB color reaction of P-CDNs modulated by different content of H3PO4, (b) A possible catalytic mechanism of P-CDNs, (c) Application of P-CDNs100 for three-mode detection of nitrite.

head.ce:title: Intensive oxidase-like activity of carbon dots nanozymes modulated with phosphoric acid for sensing

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources
School of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development and modulation of carbon dots based nanozymes (CDNs) with pleasing oxidase-like activities remains a challenging and meaningful endeavor. Herein, aiming at enhanced photocatalytic capabilities of CDNs, a phosphoric acid (H3PO4) synthesis strategy was proposed through series of remarkable CDNs development and elaboration, named as P-CDNs. In this modulated strategy, reagent of H3PO4 not only provides P atoms into the carbon dots’ backbone, generating defects for π-electron; but also functionalizes as interbedded phosphate, providing active electron sites to further promote electron efficacy and oxidase-like activities. The capacity of catalytic TMB was used to exemplify the oxidase-like activities of CDNs, and the results confirmed that the catalytic performance of P-CDNs was 3.25 times than that of the reference CDs. Based on the crackajack oxidase-like activity of P-CDNs, a smartphone remote analysis system was readily constructed to sensitive colorimetric/fluorescence dual mode response to diazotization reaction represented by nitrite. Our synthesis strategy not only provides guidance for the design of highly active CDNs but also offers directions for synthetic chemistry and materials science.

Schematic diagram of phosphoric acid modulation of PCDNs oxidase-like activity and its application in nitrite sensing.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes are nanomaterials based natural enzyme mimics that can catalyze the substrates of natural enzymes, since the peroxidase mimicking activity of Fe3O4 was first reported [1], many nanomaterials have been proved to have inherent simulated enzyme activity, such as superoxide dismutase, or hydrogenase, oxidase, etc. [2]. In recent years, nanozymes have flourished in synthetic chemistry and material science due to the excellent mimetic enzyme activity, flexibility in artificial design, and stability in harsh environments, [3,4]. Excitingly, the carbon-based nanomaterials have received increasing attention due to their rich surface functional groups, good biocompatibility, high stability, and excellent optical properties and electron transfer capabilities [5]. Among them, the carbon dots nanozymes (CDNs), a class of materials smaller than 10 nm, which has received high attention due to its low cost, easy synthesis and environmental friendliness. [6]. Notwithstanding, the diversity of precursor and synthetic additives usually lead to a wide range of uncertain performances for CDNs, i.e., lower catalytic efficiency and poorly defined active sites comparing to metal-based catalysts [7,8]. Therefore, a general adjuvant for modulating CDNs to further improve the catalytic activities during the synthesis is necessary and meaningful for synthetic chemistry and material science.

It has been fully confirmed that the catalytic activities of carbon-based materials are highly related to their specific configurations and bonding arrangements of crystal nucleus. Thus, various approaches based on heteroatom doping which can break up the regular graphitic layer and encourage charge transfer between heteroatoms and adjacent carbon atoms have been established to develop expected structures of carbon dots [9–11]. These promising strategies mostly render expected defects in the carbon dots and induce electron redistribution in the surrounding π-π conjugated system, leading to improved catalytic performance [11,12]. For instance, the insertion of B and S elements in the crystal nucleus of carbon dots enriches the material defects and inhomogeneous charge distributions, achieved significantly enhanced electrocatalytic and photocatalytic activities, respectively [13,14]. Similarly, by introducing P atoms into the crystal, more defective sites and enhanced catalytic activities have been readily developed with consequently changing on the electronic structures [15,16]. Notably, different from other heteroatoms, the P element is an excellent n-type donor, and the extra free electrons can help to improve the conductivity of hetero-doped material, favoring electron transfer and further improving the catalytic activities [17]. As reported, Pan et al., [18] confirmed that the introduction of P atoms can significantly change the electronic structure of N, P-CD (O-phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid as precursors), reduce the carrier complex and enhance the photoelectric conversion performance. More interestingly, a recent study verified that the phosphate modification effectively improved the photocatalytic solubilization efficiency of TiO2, owing to the migration of photogenerated holes from negative charge of
PO 4
3 ‐
group, as well as adsorption of oxygen to facilitate the generation of
O 2
· ‐
[19]. On the other side, Zhao et al., [20] demonstrated that the surface phosphates could improve the electrocatalysis of hetero-doped CDs and work as a highly efficient and stable electrocatalyst. Based on above achievements on the development of CDs and the inherent natures of phosphate modification, phosphates doping should be a promising direction to achieve expected catalytic activities and further progress of CDNs.

In this study, aiming at enhanced photocatalytic synthesis of CDNs, a phosphoric acid (H3PO4) synthesis strategy was developed through series of remarkable CDNs development and elaboration named as P-CDNs. Based on the observations and simulations of HR-TEM, Raman, FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, confirmed the modulation of H3PO4 not only provides P atoms into the carbon dots backbone, inducing defects; it also functionalizes as an interbedded phosphate to further promote oxidase-like activities in response to a UV light (365 nm) irritation. The oxidase-like activity was reflected by its ability to catalyze the TMB, and the results showed that the catalytic activity of P-CDNs was 3.25 times than that of the reference CDs. A smartphone remote analysis system to monitor diazotization reaction represented by nitrite was constructed based on crackajack oxidase-like activities of P-CDNs (Scheme 1 ). This strategy not only provides guidance for the design of highly active CDNs but also opens the way for nanozymes-based portable sensor applications.

All reagents were commercially purchased and used without additional purification. AR grade chemicals of ethylenediamine, phosphoric acid, 3,3′,5,5′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), ethanol, agarose, methanol, isopropanol, tryptophan, 1,4-benzoquinone, and cupric chloride (CuCl2) were provided from Aladdin reagent company (Shanghai, China). Glucose, disodium hydrogen phosphate, citric acid, NaNO2 and other chemicals containing interference ions (
Cu
2 +
,
Ca
2 +
,
Fe
3 +
,
Mg
2 +
,
K
+
,
CO 3
2 ‐
,
SO
4
2 ‐
,
NO 3 ‐
,
PO 4
3 ‐
,
Cl ‐
) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm, 25oC) employed in this study was prepared with a Millipore water purification system (Millipore, USA).

Morphology of particles was analyzed by Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-F200). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer. Power X-ray diffraction was achieved by a D2 PHASER power diffractometer, operating with Cu Kα source (Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Nicolet IS10 spectrometer. Raman spectra were collected on a Dxr2xi spectrometer with an excitation laser at 532 nm, 20 mw (Thermo Fisher, USA). Measurement of UV–vis absorption spectra was carried out on a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and the fluorescence spectra were measured on a F-700 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan). ESR spectra was measured on a EMXplus-6/1 (Bruker, Germany) and FLS1000 (Edinburgh Instruments, Britain) was used to determination of fluorescence lifetime.

The proposed P-CDNs were synthesized via a one-step microwave method. Briefly, a 400 mg of glucose was fully dissolved in 10 mL of water, then ethylenediamine (50 μL) and phosphoric acid (20, 50, 100 μL) were added and mixed for 30 min with through stirring. It was then transferred to a 700 W microwave oven for 5 min to obtain a dark brown solid. After adding an appropriate amount of water to redissolve, the unreacted precursor in the sample was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was collected after filtering with 0.22 μm membrane. Further, it was dialyzed (1000 Da) for 4 h, dried and stored for use. The synthesize of reference CDs was the same as above procedure but the absence of phosphoric acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: P-CDNs nanozymes activity assay and stead-state kinetic studies
ce:section.ce:para:
Chromogenic substrates TMB was performed to evaluate the nanozymes activity of P-CDNs. The correlation between reaction rate and substrate concentration is characterized by the classical Michaelis Menten enzyme kinetic model.
Eq. (1)
V =
V max
×
[ S ]
k m
+
[ S ]
where Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity, reflecting the catalytic activity, Km is the Michaelis constant, and [S] is the substrate concentration, which represents the affinity to the substrate. Vmax and Km values could be plotted by Lineweaver Burk's double reciprocal plotting:
Eq. (2)
1 V
=
k m
V max
×
1
[ S ]
+
1
V max
where, Vmax and Km values can be derived by plotting a linear fit of the inverse of the reaction rate (1/V) to the inverse of the substrate concentration (1/[S]) and calculating the slope and intercept.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode based on diazotization reaction
ce:section.ce:para: The diazotization reaction was verified through the reaction of P-CDNs catalyze TMB products with different concentrations of nitrite. The integral procedure is described as follows: a 10 μL of TMB (20 mM) and a 100 μL of P-CDNs100 were added to an 840 μL of phosphate buffer (pH = 4). Sequentially, the solution was quickly reacted under UV light irradiation (365 nm) for 60 s, producing a blue oxidized product, and different concentrations of nitrite solution were added to the above mixtures incubating for 15 min. Finally, the absorbance at 652 and 450 nm and the intensity of fluorescent at the wavelength of 445 nm were recorded for evaluation. The ratio of A652/A450 and intensities of FL445 were taken as their response signals for the response to nitrite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of agarose hydrogel and RGB analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, an 8 mg of agarose was dissolved in 8 mL of pure water first, then a 1 mL of P-CDNs (1 mg/mL) was added and the mixed solution was treated with micro-oven (350 W) for 30 s to ensure dissolution. After that, a 90 μL of TMB (20 mM) was added when the above solution cooling to 60oC. Finally, a hydrogel in 96-well plate was acquired following transferring the solution and left at room temperature for 5 min. After it solidified completely, the hydrogel was irradiated with 365 nm UV light for 60 s, and different concentrations of aqueous nitrite solution were introduced and reacted at room temperature for 15 min. After a picture was taken with a smartphone, the RGB analysis was operated with a Color Detector App.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of as-prepared CDs and P-CDNs
ce:section.ce:para: To elaborate the modulation of H3PO4 molecules during the synthesis of CDNs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to observe the morphology of as-prepared reference CDs and P-CDNs. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, the reference CDs which were developed without H3PO4 solution display a uniform particle with a diameter of 3.25 ± 0.5 nm, while the size of H3PO4 modulated carbon dots (P-CNDs100) increased to 4.5 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. 1c and d). Notwithstanding, high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images indicate a same lattice spacing of 0.21 nm for both carbon dots, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [21,22]. Moreover, the wide bulge at ca. 20° in X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, corresponding to the (002) planes of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1e), confirmed the amorphous carbonaceous structure of all samples [23,24]. Comparing to P-CDNs100, the reference CDs hold more regular crystal streaks, suggesting a more ordered internal structure. Meanwhile, the shift and decreased intensity in XRD patterns following the increased content of H3PO4 solution, suggesting a breaking of the π-π stacking in carbon dots by the modulation of H3PO4 molecules [25].

To distinguish the phase states of graphitic nucleus, Raman spectra measured for the D (∼1350 cm−1) and G bands (∼1600 cm−1) of CDs. It is noted that the D band arises from the out-of-plane vibrations of sp
2 C in the disordered state, corresponding to the topological sp
3 molecular defect state, while the G band arises from the in-plane stretching vibrations of sp
2 C atoms in aromatic domains [26]. Thus, the ratios of IG/ID could reasonably display the proportion of aromatic and disordered structures within the carbon dots [27]. As plotted in Fig. 1f, the modulated carbon dots tend to develop more disordered phases as evidenced by the decrease of IG/ID from 1.24 for CDs to 1.01 for P-CDNs50 with the introduction of H3PO4 solution. Such result is coincided with the observations in XRD and TEM measurements, confirming the development of disordered internal structure in carbon dots after introduction of H3PO4 molecules during the synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of H3PO4 modulated P-CDNs
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic activity was investigated for revealing the modulation of H3PO4 molecules on carbon-based nanozymes through directly catalyzing TMB oxidation. Prior to evaluating the catalytic activity, we systematically optimized the PCDNs catalytic system, with particular focus on pH and irradiation duration. The optimal conditions were determined to be pH 4 with a 60 s irradiation time (Fig. S1a and b). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the P-CDNs display remarkably active in catalyzing the model reaction and generate an intense blue-colored solution (oxTMB) with a characteristic absorption peak located at 652 nm. The enzyme-like activity was further confirmed by oxidizing two other typical oxidase substrates, 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) (Fig. 2b). As expected, the enzyme-like activity of P-CDNs displayed a clear relevance to the presence and absence of different content of H3PO4 molecules. As shown in Fig. 2c, the peak intensity at 652 nm of reference CDs was much weaker than that of H3PO4 modulated P-CDNs, moreover, the higher content of H3PO4 solution is followed by the stronger peak intensity.

To further quantify the catalytic efficiencies, stead-state kinetic assay was studied with TMB as substrate, meanwhile, the maximum initial velocity (Vmax) and Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) were calculated by plotting the Lineweaver-Burk curve according to Michaelis-Menten equation (Fig. 2d and Fig. S2). As plotted in Fig. 2e, the reference CDs display a smallest Vmax, compared with the P-CDNs. In detail, the P-CDNs100 displayed 3.25-fold catalytic efficiency than reference CDs, i.e., 25 × 10−8 M/s vs 7.69 × 10−8 M/s. On the other side, our as-prepared P-CDNs100 display a much better catalytic activity based on the kinetic characterization parameters by further comparing with other nanozymes (Table S1). Interestingly, the phospho-regulated different precursor carbon dots were also studied, and as guessed they also show different degrees of oxidase-like enhanced activities (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced mechanism and effects of P-CDNs100 nanozymes from H3PO4 modulation
ce:section.ce:para:
Active species capture experiments were performed to identify the reaction intermediates of P-CDNs nanozymes. Methanol, isopropanol, tryptophan, p-benzoquinone were served as h+, •OH, 1O2, and
O 2
· ‐
, respectively, which are the typical reactive species in nanozymes reactions [28,29]. The crucial role of
O 2
· ‐
were confirmed in catalytic system due to the nearly complete inhibited catalytic activity of P-CDNs100 by the scavenger p-benzoquinone (Fig. 2e). As indicated in Fig. 2f, the catalytic colorimetric reaction of P-CDNs100 is pretty obvious and a strong absorption peak is observed at 652 nm in oxygen atmosphere. Reversely, there is only a slight color change for the reference system at N2 atmosphere, while the weak catalytic reaction probably is supposed to be caused by the residual O2 dissolved in the system. To further elucidate the catalytic mechanism, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra was performed as shown in Fig. 2g. Comparing to the dark control group, the illuminated group exhibited distinct radical signals captured by DMPO, which are attributed to the superoxide radicals. These results provide compelling evidence that the photocatalytic process mediated by CDs operates through light-induced generation of superoxide anions.

Generally, the catalytic activity of carbon dots is primarily attributed to photoexcitation-induced electron-hole separation, where the reaction occurs when the hole traps O2 and generates
O 2
· ‐
. For our as-prepared CDs, it can be observed that the UV–Vis absorption of reference CDs in aqueous solution display a typical peak at ca. 280 nm probably corresponding to the π → π∗ transition of CC, CN or NP [30]. It can be seen that the absorption peak of the π → π∗ bond undergoes a small blue shift from 290 to 276 nm, which may be due to the change in the electronic energy level induced by the H3PO4 modulation. However, the weak peak at 346 nm sharply strengthens with increasing of H3PO4 content in the synthesis solution (Fig. 2h) due to the excited defective surface states caused by P-doping [31,32]. Such result is consistent with the Raman results of smaller IG/ID ratios for P-CDNs, implying the generation of more disordered phase structures in P-CDNs. In addition, the effect of H3PO4 modulation on electronic properties of P-CDNs was investigated by evaluating the efficiency of charge separation and complexation in CDs with fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 2i illustrates the fluorescence spectra of reference CDs and P-CDNs samples under 365 nm excitation at room temperature. It can be observed that the fluorescence intensity is obviously weakened with the increase of H3PO4 content in synthesis solution, indicating that the addition of H3PO4 content in synthesis solution is favorable to inhibit the electron-hole complexation [33].

Further analysis of the fine bonding states and chemical compositions in different content of H3PO4 solution modulated P-CDNs were investigated through XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. S4, the H3PO4 modulated P-CDNs illustrate two fitted peaks attributed to P–C (132.7–133.2 eV) and P–O (133.6–134.2 eV), respectively [34]. Meanwhile, following previous reports [34,35], the high resolution N1s spectra were further deconvoluted into three main signals corresponding to pyridinic N (399.4–399.7 eV), pyrrolic N (400.2–401.3 eV), and graphitic N (401.3–402.3 eV) species, respectively (Fig. 3 ). Noteworthily, it was observed that the proportion of pyrrolic N is significantly larger than that of the reference CDs in the P-CDNs nanozymes, and rises with increasing content of H3PO4 in the synthesis solution (Fig. 3a–d). As illustrated in Fig. 3e, the relative ratio of pyrrolic N is 33 % and 36 % for P-CDNs50 and P-CDNs100, respectively, significantly higher than that of reference CDs (18 %) and P-CDNs20 (24 %, Fig. 2e). Notably, the levels of pyrrolic N in carbon materials tends to be positively correlated with defects due to the fact that five-membered ring structure of the pyrrolic N exists only at the edges of the skeleton or at the location of defects within carbon skeleton [36]. Meanwhile, our results are fully consistent with previous investigations [37], that is, due to the lower total energy compared to other configurations of nitrogen (pyridine N, graphite N), P-doping hollow carbon nanospheres are more likely to form pyrrolic N.

Experimentally, recorded FT-IR spectra show several new peaks at 1110 cm−1 and 940 cm−1 due to phosphate ions [38,39], in addition to the stretching of O–H at 3360 cm−1 [40] (Fig. 3f). Most importantly, the peaks associated with
H 2 PO
4 ‐
, the bands at 1110 cm−1 due to the symmetric stretching vibrations of PO2, and 940 cm−1 attributed to symmetric stretching vibrations of P-(OH)2 [41], evidently raise following the increased H3PO4 in the synthesis system. Furthermore, the gradually red shift from 3390 cm−1 to 3360 cm−1 attributed to the stretching of O–H group from reference CDS to P-CDNs50 indicates a gradually generation of multiple bonding of O–H groups. Thus, according to these observations, it is reasonable to speculate that H3PO4 modulated CDs may generate several of phosphate ions in the as-prepared P-CDNs. In the synthesis and material science, it is noted that the phosphate ion is strong negative charged [42], which not only has an ideal adsorption effect on oxygen, but also could expedited photogenerated holes migration under photoactivation, promoting electron-hole separation19. Generally, it is reasonable to conclude that although H3PO4 induces more defects through doping in the core of P-CDNs, it simultaneously provides shelter for pyrrolic N and better trapping the oxygen to generate ROS. Meanwhile, with the new generation of O–H bonding sites combined with possible phosphate-negative electron regions, which allows for the facilitation of O2 to
O 2
· ‐
, the modulation ultimately evolves into an enhancement of the catalytic activities of PCDNs under irradiation (Fig. 4 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode and hydrogel sensor for diazotization reaction
ce:section.ce:para: Diazotization reaction refers to the reaction of aromatic primary amines with nitrous acid, which occupies an important position in the chemical field and is widely used in organic synthesis, biomedicine and other fields. For food sector, azo compounds are used in packaging materials the synthesis of certain pesticides such as herbicides and insecticides rely on diazotization, and safety analysis [43–45], so it is necessary to construct a sensor response to diazotization reaction. Nitrite, a typical representative of diazotization reactants, is also a hazardous substance that poses a threat to human health and is widely found in foods such as overnight meals, salted vegetables, preservatives and coloring agents. Therefore, taking nitrite as an example, the response performance of the sensor was evaluated. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, by adding different concentrations of nitrite to the oxTMB solution, it showed a clear color transition from green to yellow corresponding to low and high concentration of nitrite, respectively. Simultaneously, the peak belong to oxTMB at 652 nm is weaking and a new peak is bulging at 445 nm, which is observed in the corresponding UV spectra. Such phenomenon can be well explained by the diazotization reaction that occurs between oxTMB and nitrite (Fig. 5b). In addition to the obvious change in solution color, the fluorescence of P-CDNs100+oxTMB system is also quenched with nitrite adding (Fig. 5c).

A thorough investigation of the fluorescence quenching was performed for exploring the possible mechanism. It is found that the fluorescence lifetime displays non-significant change in the presence and absence of
NO 2 ‐
, measuring 3.44 ns and 3.75 ns, respectively (Fig. 5d). This observation rules out the possibilities of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and dynamic quenching [46]. As shown in Fig. 5e, the overlap between the UV absorption of diazotized oxTMB and the emission peak of PCDNs100 suggests the possible occurrence of an inner filter effect (IFE) [47]. The Parker equation was performed to verify the IFE [48], however, the results demonstrated that the IFE was only significant at very low concentrations, i.e., ≤20 μM, with an inhibition rate (E%) of 43.69 % (Table S2), suggesting the potential existence of other more dominant quenching mechanisms. The zeta potentials of PCDNs100, PCDNs100+oxTMB, and PCDNs100+oxTMB+
NO 2 ‐
all exhibited positive charges, measuring +4.79, +6.00, and +5.97 mV, respectively (Fig. S5). These measurements imply the presence of electrostatic repulsion between them, suggesting that charge transfer is not the quenching mechanism [49]. Based on the above results, we assume static quenching is the predominant quenching mechanism, and the Stern-Volmer analysis of static quenching revealed a strong linear correlation between F0/F and
NO 2 ‐
concentration (Fig. 5f), providing compelling evidence for static quenching. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching also can be determined by the quenching constant (Kq) calculated with the equations as follows [50,51]:
Eq. (3)
Kq =
K sv
τ 0
where τ0 is fluorescence lifetime. The KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, which is explored by:
Eq. (4)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K sv
[ C ]
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of
NO 2 ‐
, and [C] is the concentration of the
NO 2 ‐
.

The plot of (F0/F) against C shows a linear range from 20 to 100 μM with a Ksv of 4.32 × 104 M−1. The τ0 of PCDNs100+oxTMB is 3.44 ns, and the estimated Kq is 1.25 × 1013 M−1 S−1. In diffusion-controlled quenching, the Kq is near 1010 M−1 S−1 for dynamic quenching [51,52], while that is several orders larger than 1010 M−1 S−1 for static quenching. According to the above calculations, it is reasonable to conclude that the mechanism by which
NO 2 ‐
quenches the fluorescence of PCDNs100+oxTMB is attributed to the inner filter effect and static quenching, moreover, the static quenching play an important role in the predominant mechanisms.

Based on the nitrite-triggered changes in colorimetry and fluorescence, a sensitive probe for dual-mode sensing of nitrite was established. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, a good relationship between the logarithm of
NO 2 ‐
concentrations and the absorbance ratios of A652/A445 in the range of 2–300 μM were obtained. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.35 μM according to the 3σ/S (Fig. 6b). Meanwhile, under identical conditions, a clear trend that fluorescence intensity decreases with increasing
NO 2 ‐
concentration (Fig. 6d and e), was fitted as Y = 12214-71.5X in the scope of 2–150 μM with a LOD of 1.5 μM. Absolutely, the established dual-mode method displays good specificity and anti-interference (Fig. 6c and f). Most importantly, in contrast to previous studies, our probe not only displays a wide linear range and low LOD (Table S3), but the bimodal ratio has more resistant to interference due to the fact that dual-mode approaches hold site-specific manner as well as could be verified and self-corrected against each other. In addition, the fluorescence stability of PCDNs100 was investigated, and only a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed after three months of storage, demonstrating its excellent stability.

To further promote the portable visualization of as-prepared PCDNs100, herein, a remote and portable on-site sensor was constructed through employing hydrogels. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a and b, the as-prepared P-CDNs100 and TMB were pre-embedded in agarose hydrogels under dark (the initial color of hydrogel sensing kit is maintained at this stage). Then, it was irradiated with UV light at 365 nm for 1 min to transform it into blue oxTMB. Following the addition of 50 μL different concentrations of
NO 2 ‐
, the color of as-prepared hydrogel matrix gradually changed from light blue to green and finally to yellow. In addition to the handy evaluation on nitrite, by integrating the as-prepared hydrogel with a smartphone, a remote assay kit was further obtained. As illustrated in Fig. 7c, after the colorimetric reaction finish, the kit was photographed with a smartphone camera, and the information of optical image is then transformed into digitized parameters with a RGB software. As expected, the relationships between
NO 2 ‐
concentrations and R, G, B values show an obvious segmented correlation, nevertheless, the G-values remain constant within a certain range. The values of B presented a good trend of decreasing gradient with increasing analyte concentration (Fig. 7d). However, the B/G values on the
NO 2 ‐
concentration indicate a good linear relationship as Y = 1.06-0.003X in the range of 2–300 μM (Fig. 7e), indicating the capability of an alternative portable kit for quantitative analysis of nitrite. Furthermore, real sample assays (Lihu Lake water, Wuxi City, China) performed with dual-mode and RGB calculate further verified the capability of our P-CDNs on accurate monitoring of nitrite. As summarized in Table S4, the colorimetric/fluorescence and RGB methods offers good recoveries (83.5 %–110 %), indicating that the established platform has potential applicability to the analysis of nitrite in complex samples. Additionally, these methods developed based on our P-CDNs were used to detect nitrite content in sausage and self-made contaminated samples (cooked vegetables stored at room temperature for two days) for compassion with specific commercial kits. In addition to a little bit higher for the evaluation values of fluoresce and RGB approaches due to multiple conversions between different signals, our laboratory developed methods provide almost same evaluation capabilities with specific commercial kits (Table S5).

In summary, we have synthesized a high-performance CDNs by modulating with H3PO4 solution (P-CDNs). The as-prepared P-CDNs exhibit a highly oxidase-like activity, and
O 2
· ‐
plays a dominant role in the catalytic reaction. More detailed characterization confirms that the enhancement of photocatalytic activity is related to the increase of defects from P-doping induced pyrrolic N. In addition, the generated phosphate ions from H3PO4 molecules, which are negatively charged active sites in the CDs, provide much smoother conversation from O2 to
O 2
· ‐
. These alterations achieved that P-CDNs possessed 3.25 times oxidase-like activity compared to the reference CDs, through evaluation of the ability to catalyze the TMB. Benefiting from the significantly elevated oxidase-like activity carbon dots a colorimetric/fluorescent bimodal analytical method was established for remote and sensitive evaluation of diazotization reaction with satisfactory results based on the specific between oxTMB and nitrite. Surely, this strategy provides a new direction for the design of boosted artificial oxidase mimics through modulation the synthesis and demonstrates their successful application in related fields such as photochemical catalysis and analytical chemistry.

Guoyong Jiang: Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yuqi Wan: Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jingkun Li: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Junjie Qi: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yukihiro Ozaki: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis. Fuwei Pi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
The data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article and supplementary information, or available from the corresponding author on request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21603087).

Mater. 362 (2019)., 45-52.
